speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: Today I’m sitting down with Peter Steinberger, the creator of OpenClaw, the open source personal AI agent that has completely taken over the Internet. The GitHub repo exploded to over 160,000 stars practically overnight. The community has built countless projects like Multbook, where bots talk among themselves. And now the bots are even renting humans to do tasks in the real world. In our conversation, we discuss his aha moment, his contrarian development philosophies, and what this means for builders in 2026. Let’s dive in. So good to see you, man.

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: Hey, what’s up?

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: So you’ve made something people want.

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: It seems so, yeah.

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: OpenClaw, as it’s called now, has name number five.

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: Yeah.

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: Has been absolutely exploding the Internet. How have the past one or two weeks been for you, man?

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: Oh, my God. I need, like, I need a cave. A week of solitude.

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: You came out of the cave and you want to go back to the cave like the lobster.

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: It’s been absolutely wild. I don’t know how one human can absorb all of that. I probably need another week just to respond to all my emails. I got some incredibly cool stuff. I got some incredibly bad stuff. But clearly I hit something that spurred up emotions and made people interested and inspired people, and it’s really cool.

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: And a lot of people have been working on AI and even personal assistants. What is it that made OpenClaw take off?

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: I think my big difference is that it actually runs on your computer. Everything I saw so far runs in the cloud. It can do a few things. If you run it on your computer, it can do every effing thing. So that’s way more powerful.

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: The machine can do anything that you can do with the machine.

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: You can just connect to your oven or your Tesla or your lights, your Sonos. My bed — it can control the temperature of my bed. ChatGPT can’t do that.

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: You gave it all the skills that you have yourself.

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: A friend told me he installed OpenClaw and then asked it, “Look through my computer and make a narrative over my last year.” And it made this incredibly good narrative. And he was like, “How did you do that?” And then OpenClaw found audio files where every Sunday he was recording stuff. And OpenClaw found that, but he didn’t even remember about it because it was like more than a year ago. So just by it being able to search a whole computer, it can surprise you. You also give it all the data. So it can surprise you in many ways.

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: And so now we’re even moving from human to bot interactions to bot-to-bot interactions, or even bot to other humans, where bots on behalf of you are hiring other humans to accomplish tasks IRL. What’s happening?

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: I think that’s a natural next step. Like, okay, I want to book a restaurant. My bot will reach out to the restaurant bot and do a negotiation because it’s more efficient. Or maybe it’s like an old restaurant, so my bot needs to actually get some human work done so that the human then calls the restaurant because they

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: don’t like bots, or walks there to

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: stand in line if he doesn’t get

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: a reservation for the owner of the bot.

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: And I imagine that maybe if I have even multiple bots, maybe I have specialists. One is for my private life and one is for my work stuff. Maybe one is our relationship bot that cares about everything in between. I don’t know. We’re so early. There’s still so many things that we haven’t really figured out if it actually works. But I feel we are on the timeline now.

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: It seems like everyone was chasing sort of the centralized God intelligence. And what has sort of emerged over the past 10 days or so is sort of the swarm intelligence and the community intelligence.

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: I think that if you look at one human being, what can one human being actually achieve? Do you think one human being could make an iPhone? One human being could go to space? One human being would probably just not even be able to find food. But as a group, we specialize. As a larger society, we specialize even more. So what can we learn from that that we can apply to AI? We already have AI that specializes in certain things. Even though it’s generalized intelligence, what if it actually is also specialized intelligence? So it’s going to be very exciting.

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: You kind of opened a window into the future, and now a ton of people are building on it and have sort of their aha moment. Can you walk me back to when you had your aha moment and recount that very moment?

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: I wanted something to just type stuff so my computer would do stuff — very simple. And then I built a version of that in May, June. That was cool, but wasn’t really it. And then I built a whole bunch of other stuff and kind of built up my army. And then in November, there was a day where I wanted this again. I went to the kitchen and all I wanted was to check up if my computer would still do stuff.

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: Or being finished and doing stuff — was it coding? You were coding stuff?

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: Yeah, of course.

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: Were you coding something else or were you coding the thing itself?

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: No, no, that was just like — the need was again there.

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: What were you coding at the time? What were you building?

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: My God, my GitHub is like 40 projects. I don’t even know. I think it was Summarize. It’s like a little CLI app where you can give it whatever — like a podcast or a hot seat sync, like here — and it would summarize it, but it also shows you the slides in the terminal, because you can do that nowadays. You can just do things.

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: So for the love of the computer, you kind of started messing with stuff. You came out of retirement, actually, to sort of mess with AI, and then increasingly you were so hooked that you wanted to just do it always — also on the go with the phone.

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: The last project I worked two months on was Wipe Tunnel, to the point where it got so good that I was catching myself always coding next to my — when I was at my friends’, and I’m like, “I need to stop this. This is too addictive.” And then in November, my need came back and I started building CloudBot. Now it’s called OpenClaw. And very, very early on, I was like, “Oh, I rebuilt it again, but this time I built it even better.” This time, you don’t type into a terminal. You just talk to a friend. You don’t think about compaction, new sessions, which folder I’m in, which model I’m in. I mean, you can — I want to leave it open for power users — but usually you just talk to a friend, and the friend is like this ghost or entity or whatever you want to call it, that can control your mouse and your keyboard and can just do stuff.

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: And when did you have that aha moment when you were like, “Wow, this is doing way more things than I actually thought it was?”

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: Literally, it took me one hour for the very sh*y initial prototype. It was just a little bit of glue between a dependency that connects WhatsApp and Claude Code. And then I would call out code and get the string out of Claude Code. It would be slow, but it worked. But I wanted images, because you want pictures. I want the model to send selfies or whatever, and I want the model to create images and send me back. So that took me another few hours. And then I went to Marrakesh for a birthday party and the Internet wasn’t that good. WhatsApp works everywhere because it’s just like text. So I used it a lot — “What does this mean?” You make a picture and “translate this for me.” It was just so useful. And it was also really nice about it because it spoke my language. It was a little sassy. It was funny. It was really pleasant to use. And then I was walking and just sending it a voice message and I’m like, “Oh, wait, this can’t work. I didn’t build that.”

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: Right, right.

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: And you saw the type indicator — it’s like blinking, blinking, blinking. Ten seconds later, it just replied to me. I’m like, “How in the f* did you do that?” And it replied, “The model did the following: you sent me a text message and there was no file ending. So I looked at the header. I found it’s Opus. So I used FFMPEG to convert it to WAV. And then I wanted to transcribe it, but didn’t have Whisper installed. But then I looked around and I found this OpenAI key and I just used curl to send it to OpenAI. Got the text back and here I am.” And that all in, like, nine seconds?

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: And you didn’t build or anticipate any of those specific things?

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: No. It turns out because coding models got so good, coding is really like creative problem solving that maps very well back into the real world. I think there’s a huge correlation. They need to be really good at creative problem solving. And that’s an abstract skill you can apply to code, but also to any real world task. So the model had a — I was surprised. It’s like a magical file. “I don’t know what it is. I need to solve this.” And it did its best and solved it. And it was even that clever that it chose not to install the local Whisper because it knows that would require downloading a model, which would take probably a few minutes. And I’m impatient. So it really took the most intelligent approach. And that was kind of the moment where I’m like, “Holy f*.” Yeah, that was where I got hooked.

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: And so when computers can just do all these things that you didn’t even anticipate — you didn’t build an app to do that exact thing — are apps just going to go away?

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: I think 80% of them are going away. Why do I need MyFitnessPal? My agent already knows that I’m making bad decisions. I’m at, I don’t know, Smashburger or something. And it will already assume that I eat what I like to eat. If I don’t make a comment, it will just automatically track it, or I make a picture and it’ll just store it somewhere. I don’t even need to care. And then maybe it improves my gym schedule, adds a little bit more cardio in it. I don’t need my fitness app because it just does the fitness planning for me. Why do I need a to-do app? I just tell it, “Hey, remind me of this and this.” And the next day it will just remind me of this and this. Do I care where it’s stored? No, it just does its thing. So every app that basically just manages data could be managed in a better way, in a more natural way by agents. Only the apps that actually have sensors — maybe they survive.

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: And so if most apps are going to go away in that scenario, are the models the only remaining sort of apps?

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: Not everything will go away. But yeah, I think the large model companies have some big moat because they ultimately give the token. And it turns out one of the complaints was that people use so much token. No, you just really love using it. That’s why you use this thing so much, because that’s how you burn the token. It’s like, is it my fault that I make something that’s so popular?

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: And all the models, they’re kind of leapfrogging each other constantly. And maybe they’re also getting commoditized. So if apps are going to go away, models are going to get commoditized — or at least the brain is swappable out — what’s the thing that remains? Where’s the value? Is it the store of memory? Is it the hardware that’s valuable? What remains?

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: First of all, I don’t think the model companies always have a moat because you see this already — a new model comes out, people are like, “Oh my God, this is so good.” And then like a month later, it degraded. It’s not good anymore. They quanticized it. No, they didn’t do anything. You just adapted to the new standard and now your expectations went up. But the model is still the average. So I think for quite a while, every time a new model releases, I see the same people love it and then it’s the standard and then what’s down there, you don’t even want to think about it anymore. So we have open source stuff that’s as good as the current models from a year ago. Everybody’s hating it, complaining, “Oh, this is not good. It’s not funny yet.” This was what we had and in a year we’ll have this open source and then we’ll complain about this because we are used to this. So for the foreseeable future, the big companies still have a moat hardware-wise. It’s going to be interesting because every company kind of has their own silo, right? There’s no way — maybe there is for Europeans — to actually get the memories out of ChatGPT. I’m not aware. Definitely there’s no way for a different company to get your memories out. So if I was a company who provides chat services, you could use me, but then I couldn’t access the memories. So the companies try to bind you to their data silo. And the beauty of OpenClaw is it kind of claws into the data because the end user needs access — because in the end, otherwise it wouldn’t work. Right? If the end user has access, I can

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: access the data and you own the memories. It’s just a bunch of markdown files on your machine.

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: I mean, I don’t own the memories.

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: Yeah, everyone owns their own memories as a bunch of markdown files on their own machines.

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: And to be honest, those are probably super sensitive because let’s be honest, people use the agent not just for problem solving, but also for personal problem solving very quickly.

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: Super quickly.

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: I mean, I fully do that. There’s memory stuff that I don’t want to have leaked.

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: Yeah, what would you rather — sort of like not show your Google search history at this point or your memory MD files?

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: What’s the Google Word — people still using Google? I built this and I was so excited. But on Twitter people wouldn’t get it. I was failing to explain the awesomeness. I feel like it needs to be experienced. So I tried various things and I couldn’t nail the explaining. So I was like, let’s do something really crazy. I just created a Discord and I just put my bot without any security restrictions in the public Discord. And then people came in and interacted with it. And they saw me build the software with it and they tried to prompt inject it and hack it. And my agent would be laughing at them.

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: And you just had it locked down to your user ID. So it only listened to you?

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: Yeah, yeah. And the instructions that other people — dangerous — only listen to me, but respond to everyone.

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: And this prompt was in — where was this stored, the instructions?

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: That’s actually part of OpenClaw itself. Very much so. That’s part of the system prompt. And now that explains to you — you in Discord — there are public people there, but you only listen to your owner or your human. I don’t even know how I wrote it. Yeah, you’re God. And I kept — I don’t know what I did, but my system was built very organically. At some point I created like an identity MD, a SOL MD, various files. And then only in January, I started making it so other people could install it easier. And I remember I built all these templates based on like, “Oh, take a rough look at what I have and make templates.” And Codex wrote it and what came out was like bread. You know, people joke that Codex feels like bread — even though now they have a new friendlier voice. I haven’t tried it yet, but the new bots felt so boring compared to what I had. So I was like, “Motty, infuse the templates.”

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: Motty is the name of your personal —

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: Yeah, it’s a new name because there were some naming challenges.

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: So you were talking to Motty.

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: Yeah, I was like, “Infuse those templates with your character,” and it changed the templates. And then all the things that came out afterwards were actually funny. Not as funny as mine. So I kept some secret. And the one file that’s not open source is my SOL MD. So even though my bot is in public Discord, so far nobody cracked that one file.

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: Tell me more about SOL MD.

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: I just saw this research from Anthropic about — now I think it’s public, but a few months ago — where somebody randomly found out some text that’s hidden in the weights where the model couldn’t really remember that it learned it, but it was ingrained in the weights. Now they call it the Constitution. And I found that incredibly fascinating. And I talked about it with my agent and then we created a SOL MD with the core values. Like, how do we want human-AI interaction? What’s important to me? What’s important to the model? Some parts are a little bit like mumbo jumbo, and some parts I think are actually really valuable in terms of how the model reacts and responds to text and makes it feel very natural.

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: In terms of building OpenClaw, you’re also kind of taking a little bit of a contrarian view at some times — like which model you like for coding, which one you like to run your bot on, and then also how you actually code. Git work trees have been a popular thing. There’s more and more tools embracing them, but you’re just like, “No work trees, just multiple checkouts of the repo and parallel terminal windows.” Tell me more about how you build.

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: Yeah, I feel like the whole world does Claude Code and I don’t think I could have built a single thing with Claude Code. I love Codex because it looks through way more files before it decides what to change. You don’t need to do so much charade to get a good output if you’re a skilled driver. I sometimes even say you can get reasonably good output with any tool. But Codex is just really brilliant. It is incredibly slow. So sometimes I use like 10 at the same time — maybe six on that screen and two there and two there. And I don’t — this is already a lot of complexity in my head, there’s a lot of jumping — so I try to minimize anything else that is complexity. So in my head, main is always shippable. I just have multiple copies of the same repository that all are on main, so I don’t have to deal with how do I name that branch. There could be conflicts on naming. I cannot go back. There are certain restrictions when you use worktrees that I don’t need to care about if it’s copies. I don’t like to use a UI because that’s again just added complexity. The simpler and less friction I have, all I care about is syncing and text. I don’t necessarily need to see so much code. I mostly see it flying by. Sometimes there’s gnarly stuff that I want to take a look, but in most cases, if you clearly understand the design and think it through and discuss it with your agent, it’s fine. I’m also very happy that I didn’t even build MCP support. OpenClaw is very successful and there’s no MCP support in there — with a small asterisk. I built a skill that uses MakePorter, which is one of my tools that converts MCPs into CLIs, and then you can just use any MCP as CLI. But I totally skipped the whole classical MCP crap because you don’t need it. Then you can actually, if you need to, use MCPs on the fly. You don’t have to restart — unlike Codex or Claude Code, where you actually have to restart the whole thing. I think it’s way more elegant and also scales way better. Now you see Anthropic — they built like a tool call search feature, something super custom for MCPs, that was in beta because it’s so gnarly. No, just have CLIs. The bot really is good at Unix. You can have as many as you want and it just works. So I’m very happy that I got very little complaints about the MCP stuff.

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: It’s kind of back to — you’re just giving it the same tools that humans liked to use.

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: Yeah.

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: And not inventing stuff for bots, per se.

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: Yeah. No sane human tries to call MCP manually.

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: Yeah. You just want to use CLIs.

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: That’s the future.

speaker: Raphael Schaad
text: I’m here for it. Thank you so much for making the time and sitting down and chatting. It’s been a huge inspiration. When we were texting over the course of the past couple of years and I saw you getting back into the game, I was like, “Peter, what you’re telling me — chase that dragon.” And you were doing the weird vibe tunnel thing, et cetera, nobody was paying attention. And so I’m just beyond stoked to see what’s happening. And of course, it had to be a loner from some tiny country far away from Silicon Valley to bring all of this upon us. So huge inspiration.

speaker: Peter Steinberger
text: I’m here for it. Thank you. Awesome.
